item.title: A cloud detection method for remote sensing images combining optical images and SAR images
item.dc:creator: Chengjie Hu
Bo Wang
Qinghong Sheng
Jun Li
Xiao Ling
Xiang Liu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Accurate cloud detection in optical remote sensing imagery remains a persistent challenge, particularly for semi-transparent thin clouds and high-reflectance terrestrial surfaces where spectral signatures often overlap. This paper proposes SAR-OPT-CDNet, a novel multimodal fusion framework that synergistically integrates optical spectral information with the cloud-penetrating structural cues from synthetic aperture radar (SAR). To effectively bridge the modality gap, we develop a Multimodal Feature Fusion Module (MFFM) that explicitly disentangles and aggregates joint representations and cross-modal differential features under a channel-spatial attention mechanism. Furthermore, we introduce Mamba-inspired Visual State Space (VSS) Blocks to facilitate efficient multi-directional contextual modeling with linear computational complexity, significantly enhancing the delineation of irregular cloud boundaries and the detection of fragmented cloud structures. Experimental results on the newly established NUAA-SAR-OPT-CD dataset—comprising temporally paired Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2 imagery—demonstrate that our method achieves a state-of-the-art overall accuracy (OA) of 99.13% and an F1-score of 0.9178. Qualitative and quantitative analyses confirm that the proposed framework substantially reduces omission errors in thin-cloud regions and suppresses false alarms over high-reflectance surfaces, offering a robust and efficient solution for complex remote sensing applications. Published: 2026-06-03T19:40:37+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.821 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Chengjie Hu; Bo Wang; Qinghong Sheng; Jun Li; Xiao Ling; Xiang Liu

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3699544

Relevance: 0.821 (must_read)

Accurate cloud detection in optical remote sensing imagery remains a persistent challenge, particularly for semi-transparent thin clouds and high-reflectance terrestrial surfaces where spectral signatures often overlap. This paper proposes SAR-OPT-CDNet, a novel multimodal fusion framework that synergistically integrates optical spectral information with the cloud-penetrating structural cues from synthetic aperture radar (SAR). To effectively bridge the modality gap, we develop a Multimodal Feature Fusion Module (MFFM) that explicitly disentangles and aggregates joint representations and cross-modal differential features under a channel-spatial attention mechanism. Furthermore, we introduce Mamba-inspired Visual State Space (VSS) Blocks to facilitate efficient multi-directional contextual modeling with linear computational complexity, significantly enhancing the delineation of irregular cloud boundaries and the detection of fragmented cloud structures. Experimental results on the newly established NUAA-SAR-OPT-CD dataset—comprising temporally paired Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2 imagery—demonstrate that our method achieves a state-of-the-art overall accuracy (OA) of 99.13% and an F1-score of 0.9178. Qualitative and quantitative analyses confirm that the proposed framework substantially reduces omission errors in thin-cloud regions and suppresses false alarms over high-reflectance surfaces, offering a robust and efficient solution for complex remote sensing applications.

item.title: TSC-DETR: DETR with Transformer-Semantics to CNN Guidance for Small Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Zheng Yu
Li Guo
Hongyuan Huo
Qing Xu
Fang Ren
Qifan Cui
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Object detection models based on the Detection Transformer (DETR) architecture suffer from a performance bottleneck due to its attention mechanism’s bias toward high-level semantic information, resulting in insufficient low-level detail feature extraction. Particularly in small object detection, existing DETR models suffer from significant deficiencies in detail representation due to the limited number of pixels in the target region. To address this issue, we propose the Transformer-Semantics to CNN Guidance DEtection TRansformer (TSC-DETR), which leverages high-level semantic guidance and a CNN to enhance low-level edge feature extraction for small object detection. First, we introduce a method to enhance the encoder’s dynamic multi-scale perception capabilities to compensate for the linear attention mechanism’s limitations in describing edge detail. Secondly, we utilize a high-level semantic prior prediction guidance map generated by the Transformer encoder to guide the convolutional network to focus more on positive sample regions. Also, to address the problem of small objects being missed due to the ”flooding effect” of high-level semantics in negative sample regions, we design a prediction guidance map specifically for negative sample regions to improve the model’s ability to recognize small objects. Furthermore, we constructed a multi-scale, multi-granularity detail perception module. This module relies on high-level semantics to focus on positive and negative regions, fusing multi-level information to enhance the expressive power of low-level features in depicting the boundaries and textures of small objects. TSC-DETR outperforms previous CNN-based and DETR-based methods, achieving an AP of 31.5% on AI-TOD and 36.7% on Visdrone2019.The project page:https://github.com/AuroraLove/tsc-detr. Published: 2026-06-03T19:40:37+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.817 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zheng Yu; Li Guo; Hongyuan Huo; Qing Xu; Fang Ren; Qifan Cui

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3699672

Relevance: 0.817 (must_read)

Object detection models based on the Detection Transformer (DETR) architecture suffer from a performance bottleneck due to its attention mechanism’s bias toward high-level semantic information, resulting in insufficient low-level detail feature extraction. Particularly in small object detection, existing DETR models suffer from significant deficiencies in detail representation due to the limited number of pixels in the target region. To address this issue, we propose the Transformer-Semantics to CNN Guidance DEtection TRansformer (TSC-DETR), which leverages high-level semantic guidance and a CNN to enhance low-level edge feature extraction for small object detection. First, we introduce a method to enhance the encoder’s dynamic multi-scale perception capabilities to compensate for the linear attention mechanism’s limitations in describing edge detail. Secondly, we utilize a high-level semantic prior prediction guidance map generated by the Transformer encoder to guide the convolutional network to focus more on positive sample regions. Also, to address the problem of small objects being missed due to the ”flooding effect” of high-level semantics in negative sample regions, we design a prediction guidance map specifically for negative sample regions to improve the model’s ability to recognize small objects. Furthermore, we constructed a multi-scale, multi-granularity detail perception module. This module relies on high-level semantics to focus on positive and negative regions, fusing multi-level information to enhance the expressive power of low-level features in depicting the boundaries and textures of small objects. TSC-DETR outperforms previous CNN-based and DETR-based methods, achieving an AP of 31.5% on AI-TOD and 36.7% on Visdrone2019.The project page:https://github.com/AuroraLove/tsc-detr.

item.title: LaVIDE: Language-Prompted Satellite Change Detection via Map-Image Alignment
item.dc:creator: Shuguo Jiang
Fang Xu
Chuandong Liu
Hong Tan
Shengyang Li
Lei Yu
Wen Yang
Sen Jia
Gui-Song Xia
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
item.description: Remote sensing change detection based on a map reference and an up-to-date image boosts timely observation of the Earth's surface when earlier images are lacking for comparison. However, the semantic gap between high-level map categories and low-level image details hinders the extraction of homogeneous features for robust temporal association in change detection. Unlike conventional approaches that either compare pixel-level visual similarity or propagate segmentation errors, we propose a novel framework, Language-VIsion Discriminator for dEtecting changes, LaVIDE, which bridges the semantic gap between high-level map categories and low-level image details using language as an intermediary. Specifically, we intro duce restricted prompt learning to generate context-aware textual prompts that align map semantics with image content, and an object-aware embedding enhancement strategy to integrate object level attributes (e.g., shape, boundary) into map representations. These components enable robust cross-modal alignment within a unified language-vision feature space. Extensive experiments on four benchmarks, DynamicEarthNet, HRSCD, BANDON, and SECOND, demonstrate that LaVIDE outperforms state-of-the art methods by significant margins, achieving 18.4% and 5.2% improvements in IoU on multi-class and single-class change detection tasks, respectively. Our framework not only advances the accuracy of map-image change detection but also provides a practical solution for rapid map updating with minimal human intervention, promising broad impacts in urban planning, disaster assessment, and ecological conservation. Code and datasets are available at: https://github.com/ShuGuoJ/LAVIDE.git. Published: 2026-06-03T19:40:23+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence Score: 0.817 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Shuguo Jiang; Fang Xu; Chuandong Liu; Hong Tan; Shengyang Li; Lei Yu; Wen Yang; Sen Jia; Gui-Song Xia

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

DOI: 10.1109/tpami.2026.3700146

Relevance: 0.817 (must_read)

Remote sensing change detection based on a map reference and an up-to-date image boosts timely observation of the Earth's surface when earlier images are lacking for comparison. However, the semantic gap between high-level map categories and low-level image details hinders the extraction of homogeneous features for robust temporal association in change detection. Unlike conventional approaches that either compare pixel-level visual similarity or propagate segmentation errors, we propose a novel framework, Language-VIsion Discriminator for dEtecting changes, LaVIDE, which bridges the semantic gap between high-level map categories and low-level image details using language as an intermediary. Specifically, we intro duce restricted prompt learning to generate context-aware textual prompts that align map semantics with image content, and an object-aware embedding enhancement strategy to integrate object level attributes (e.g., shape, boundary) into map representations. These components enable robust cross-modal alignment within a unified language-vision feature space. Extensive experiments on four benchmarks, DynamicEarthNet, HRSCD, BANDON, and SECOND, demonstrate that LaVIDE outperforms state-of-the art methods by significant margins, achieving 18.4% and 5.2% improvements in IoU on multi-class and single-class change detection tasks, respectively. Our framework not only advances the accuracy of map-image change detection but also provides a practical solution for rapid map updating with minimal human intervention, promising broad impacts in urban planning, disaster assessment, and ecological conservation. Code and datasets are available at: https://github.com/ShuGuoJ/LAVIDE.git.

item.title: Generalizable Unsupervised Array-SAR Restoration with Dual-Stage Refinement
item.dc:creator: Yangyang Wang
Zhiming He
Xu Zhan
Chudi Zhang
Yuxuan Liu
Shunjun Wei
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
item.description: Array synthetic aperture radar (array-SAR) systems are increasingly employed for scene sensing, such as security inspection and close-range restoration, owing to their ability to exploit spatial diversity for enhanced resolution. However, the performance of traditional restoration methods is often limited by measurement noise, sparse sampling, and the reliance on large amounts of labeled datasets. Although regularization based methods can improve SAR image quality to a certain extent, they suffer from difficulties in designing appropriate regularization functions for different features of targets and in tuning associated parameters. These constraints limit their applicability in practical scenarios. To address above challenges, we propose a generalizable unsupervised restoration method for array-SAR with a two-stage coarse-to-fine refinement network driven by range-null space decomposition. The proposed framework initially extracts low-dimensional range components, followed by high-fidelity restoration using a cascaded encoder-decoder architecture. To enable unsupervised model learning, an unsupervised loss function is incorporated to enforce consistency with the measurement data. Furthermore, a recorruption-based strategy is integrated to generate virtual samples during training, effectively mimicking the advantages of supervised learning while eliminating the dependence on ground truth. Extensive simulations and real-world experiments demonstrate that the proposed method exhibits strong adaptability across diverse environments and target types, particularly in scenarios involving distributed or non-point-like targets. Published: 2026-06-02T20:09:02+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems Score: 0.814 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yangyang Wang; Zhiming He; Xu Zhan; Chudi Zhang; Yuxuan Liu; Shunjun Wei

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems

DOI: 10.1109/taes.2026.3699494

Relevance: 0.814 (must_read)

Array synthetic aperture radar (array-SAR) systems are increasingly employed for scene sensing, such as security inspection and close-range restoration, owing to their ability to exploit spatial diversity for enhanced resolution. However, the performance of traditional restoration methods is often limited by measurement noise, sparse sampling, and the reliance on large amounts of labeled datasets. Although regularization based methods can improve SAR image quality to a certain extent, they suffer from difficulties in designing appropriate regularization functions for different features of targets and in tuning associated parameters. These constraints limit their applicability in practical scenarios. To address above challenges, we propose a generalizable unsupervised restoration method for array-SAR with a two-stage coarse-to-fine refinement network driven by range-null space decomposition. The proposed framework initially extracts low-dimensional range components, followed by high-fidelity restoration using a cascaded encoder-decoder architecture. To enable unsupervised model learning, an unsupervised loss function is incorporated to enforce consistency with the measurement data. Furthermore, a recorruption-based strategy is integrated to generate virtual samples during training, effectively mimicking the advantages of supervised learning while eliminating the dependence on ground truth. Extensive simulations and real-world experiments demonstrate that the proposed method exhibits strong adaptability across diverse environments and target types, particularly in scenarios involving distributed or non-point-like targets.

item.title: GVHMR: Gravity-View Coordinates for Global Human Motion Recovery From Monocular Videos
item.dc:creator: Zehong Shen
Jiawei Wang
Huaijin Pi
Yan Xia
Zhi Cen
Zechen Hu
Hujun Bao
Ruizhen Hu
Xiaowei Zhou
Sida Peng
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
item.description: We present a novel method for recovering world-grounded human motion from monocular video. The main challenge lies in the ambiguity of defining the world coordinate system, which varies between sequences. Previous approaches attempt to alleviate this issue by predicting relative motion in an auto-regressive manner, but are prone to accumulating errors. Instead, we propose estimating human poses in a novel Gravity-View (GV) coordinate system, which is defined by the world gravity and the camera view direction. The proposed GV system is naturally gravity-aligned and uniquely defined for each video frame, largely reducing the ambiguity of learning image-pose mapping. The estimated poses can be transformed back to the world coordinate system using camera rotations, forming a global motion sequence. Additionally, the per-frame estimation avoids error accumulation in the auto-regressive methods. Experiments on in-the-wild benchmarks demonstrate that our method recovers more realistic motion in both the camera space and world-grounded settings, outperforming state-of-the-art methods in both accuracy and speed. We further introduce a Stationary Label Predictor (SLP) to estimate the motion stationary states of hands and feet. By producing sharper decision boundaries, the module mitigates unnatural artifacts such as foot-sliding and shows improved performance under fast-motion inputs. The code is available at https://zju3dv.github.io/gvhmr. Published: 2026-06-03T19:40:23+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence Score: 0.812 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zehong Shen; Jiawei Wang; Huaijin Pi; Yan Xia; Zhi Cen; Zechen Hu; Hujun Bao; Ruizhen Hu; Xiaowei Zhou; Sida Peng

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

DOI: 10.1109/tpami.2026.3699758

Relevance: 0.812 (must_read)

We present a novel method for recovering world-grounded human motion from monocular video. The main challenge lies in the ambiguity of defining the world coordinate system, which varies between sequences. Previous approaches attempt to alleviate this issue by predicting relative motion in an auto-regressive manner, but are prone to accumulating errors. Instead, we propose estimating human poses in a novel Gravity-View (GV) coordinate system, which is defined by the world gravity and the camera view direction. The proposed GV system is naturally gravity-aligned and uniquely defined for each video frame, largely reducing the ambiguity of learning image-pose mapping. The estimated poses can be transformed back to the world coordinate system using camera rotations, forming a global motion sequence. Additionally, the per-frame estimation avoids error accumulation in the auto-regressive methods. Experiments on in-the-wild benchmarks demonstrate that our method recovers more realistic motion in both the camera space and world-grounded settings, outperforming state-of-the-art methods in both accuracy and speed. We further introduce a Stationary Label Predictor (SLP) to estimate the motion stationary states of hands and feet. By producing sharper decision boundaries, the module mitigates unnatural artifacts such as foot-sliding and shows improved performance under fast-motion inputs. The code is available at https://zju3dv.github.io/gvhmr.

item.title: Optical-Guided Neural Collapse for SAR Few-Shot Class Incremental Learning
item.dc:creator: Fan Zhang
Sijin Zheng
Fei Ma
Qiang Yin
Yongsheng Zhou
Fei Gao
Xian Sun
item.prism:publicationName: arXiv
item.description: Few-shot class-incremental learning (FSCIL) in synthetic aperture radar imagery presents unique challenges due to severe data scarcity and SAR-specific variability. In particular, strong azimuth sensitivity in SAR induces large intra-class variation and inter-class confusion, and FSCIL sequential updates further lead to catastrophic forgetting of previously learned classes. Inspired by neural collapse, we propose an optical-guided SAR FSCIL framework, which derives orthogonal feature subspaces from a data-rich optical ATR dataset and uses them as geometric priors to guide SAR feature learning. SAR features are projected onto these orthogonal subspaces via principal angle constraints, effectively transferring discriminative structure from the optical to the SAR domain. Specifically, our projection loss and the classifier loss optimized with a frozen simplex-ETF geometry jointly induce neural collapse by concentrating features around class means while maintaining large inter-class angles. We evaluate the approach on a benchmark comprising an optical ATR dataset and a SAR ATR dataset with 24 target classes, organized into a base training session and seven incremental sessions. Compared with recent FSCIL methods including NCFSCIL and so on, our method achieves the highest final accuracy and a favorable trade-off between final performance and performance degradation. Moreover, neural collapse metrics show improved intra-class compactness and inter-class separability, indicating that the learned features more closely approximate the ideal simplex-ETF geometry. Published: 2026-06-03T07:09:20+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.810 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Fan Zhang; Sijin Zheng; Fei Ma; Qiang Yin; Yongsheng Zhou; Fei Gao; Xian Sun

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.810 (must_read)

Few-shot class-incremental learning (FSCIL) in synthetic aperture radar imagery presents unique challenges due to severe data scarcity and SAR-specific variability. In particular, strong azimuth sensitivity in SAR induces large intra-class variation and inter-class confusion, and FSCIL sequential updates further lead to catastrophic forgetting of previously learned classes. Inspired by neural collapse, we propose an optical-guided SAR FSCIL framework, which derives orthogonal feature subspaces from a data-rich optical ATR dataset and uses them as geometric priors to guide SAR feature learning. SAR features are projected onto these orthogonal subspaces via principal angle constraints, effectively transferring discriminative structure from the optical to the SAR domain. Specifically, our projection loss and the classifier loss optimized with a frozen simplex-ETF geometry jointly induce neural collapse by concentrating features around class means while maintaining large inter-class angles. We evaluate the approach on a benchmark comprising an optical ATR dataset and a SAR ATR dataset with 24 target classes, organized into a base training session and seven incremental sessions. Compared with recent FSCIL methods including NCFSCIL and so on, our method achieves the highest final accuracy and a favorable trade-off between final performance and performance degradation. Moreover, neural collapse metrics show improved intra-class compactness and inter-class separability, indicating that the learned features more closely approximate the ideal simplex-ETF geometry.

item.title: Global–Local Interaction and Recalibration Network for Salient Object Detection in Optical Remote Sensing Images
item.dc:creator: Xiaofei Zhou
Chenwei Zhu
Liuxin Bao
Deyang Liu
Bolun Zheng
Runmin Cong
Jiyong Zhang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Cybernetics
item.description: Optical remote sensing images (RSIs) exhibit extensive spatial coverage and complex geographic backgrounds, where salient objects in the optical RSIs present a variety of scales, shapes, and orientations. Over the past few years, many deep learning-based approaches, including convolutional neural network (CNN)-based and Transformer-based models, have been devoted to salient object detection (SOD) in optical RSIs. However, those models do not fully explore the complementarity and differences between CNN and Transformer features, resulting in a degradation in performance. Therefore, in this article, we propose a global–local interaction and recalibration network (i.e., GLIR-Net), which leverages the complementarity of global context from Transformer and local details from CNN while harmonizing their inherent differences. Specifically, in the encoder, we use the two-branch architecture comprising a Transformer branch and a CNN branch to extract the global contextual and local detailed features, respectively. Second, we employ the multiscale feature interaction (MFI) module to promote the interaction of the CNN and Transformer features, where the two features are mutually enhanced by their complementarity. Third, the two kinds of features are aggregated via the feature recalibration module (FRM), where the CNN and Transformer features are fused and recalibrated to harmonize their inherent differences. Finally, by deploying the decoder, we can progressively acquire the high-quality saliency map for each optical RSI. We conduct extensive comparative experiments with state-of-the-art models on two public datasets, and the experimental results firmly demonstrate that the proposed GLIR-Net outperforms all the other competitors both qualitatively and quantitatively. Published: 2026-06-02T20:07:22+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Cybernetics Score: 0.809 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xiaofei Zhou; Chenwei Zhu; Liuxin Bao; Deyang Liu; Bolun Zheng; Runmin Cong; Jiyong Zhang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Cybernetics

DOI: 10.1109/tcyb.2026.3696712

Relevance: 0.809 (must_read)

Optical remote sensing images (RSIs) exhibit extensive spatial coverage and complex geographic backgrounds, where salient objects in the optical RSIs present a variety of scales, shapes, and orientations. Over the past few years, many deep learning-based approaches, including convolutional neural network (CNN)-based and Transformer-based models, have been devoted to salient object detection (SOD) in optical RSIs. However, those models do not fully explore the complementarity and differences between CNN and Transformer features, resulting in a degradation in performance. Therefore, in this article, we propose a global–local interaction and recalibration network (i.e., GLIR-Net), which leverages the complementarity of global context from Transformer and local details from CNN while harmonizing their inherent differences. Specifically, in the encoder, we use the two-branch architecture comprising a Transformer branch and a CNN branch to extract the global contextual and local detailed features, respectively. Second, we employ the multiscale feature interaction (MFI) module to promote the interaction of the CNN and Transformer features, where the two features are mutually enhanced by their complementarity. Third, the two kinds of features are aggregated via the feature recalibration module (FRM), where the CNN and Transformer features are fused and recalibrated to harmonize their inherent differences. Finally, by deploying the decoder, we can progressively acquire the high-quality saliency map for each optical RSI. We conduct extensive comparative experiments with state-of-the-art models on two public datasets, and the experimental results firmly demonstrate that the proposed GLIR-Net outperforms all the other competitors both qualitatively and quantitatively.

item.title: Two-timer-KAN: Dual-Exclusive Fourier KANs with Gaussian Fusion for Few-Shot Multimodal Remote Sensing Imagery Classification
item.dc:creator: Jiaojiao Li
Hailong Wu
Haitao Xu
Rui Song
Yunsong Li
Qian Du
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
item.description: Multimodal remote sensing imagery classification (MRSIC) aims to synergistically leverage complementary information from heterogeneous data sources, enabling precise land-cover classification. Existing MRSIC approaches predominantly rely on abundant annotated samples, facing critical performance degradation under data-scarce scenarios that are particularly exacerbated by the inherent complexity of heterogeneous multi-modal data. Furthermore, effectively extracting spatial-spectral information of multimodal data and fusing the cross-modal heterogeneous features persists as a significant challenge. To address these obstacles, we propose a pioneering few-shot MRSIC network, Two-timer-KAN, which integrates modality-specific feature extraction for spectral- and spatial-dominant data. Specifically, leveraging the nonlinear power of Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs), we develop the Dual-Exclusive Fourier KAN (DEF-KAN) encoder, which captures modality-specific global features in the frequency domain, bridging spectral and spatial gaps across various datasets. Following this, a Multivariate-Gaussian-based Cross-KAN (MG-Cross-KAN) is dedicated to enhancing the robustness of cross-modality fusion by capturing modality-shared features in a distribution-based manner. Finally, to further tackle classification ambiguity under limited annotated samples, we present a visual-textual bidirectional alignment strategy, which leverages textual descriptions as supplementary semantical knowledge to clarify class feature centers. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed Two-timer-KAN achieves superior performance, outperforming the state-of-the-art methods in both accuracy and robustness. Published: 2026-06-02T20:09:12+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.808 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jiaojiao Li; Hailong Wu; Haitao Xu; Rui Song; Yunsong Li; Qian Du

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3697646

Relevance: 0.808 (must_read)

Multimodal remote sensing imagery classification (MRSIC) aims to synergistically leverage complementary information from heterogeneous data sources, enabling precise land-cover classification. Existing MRSIC approaches predominantly rely on abundant annotated samples, facing critical performance degradation under data-scarce scenarios that are particularly exacerbated by the inherent complexity of heterogeneous multi-modal data. Furthermore, effectively extracting spatial-spectral information of multimodal data and fusing the cross-modal heterogeneous features persists as a significant challenge. To address these obstacles, we propose a pioneering few-shot MRSIC network, Two-timer-KAN, which integrates modality-specific feature extraction for spectral- and spatial-dominant data. Specifically, leveraging the nonlinear power of Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs), we develop the Dual-Exclusive Fourier KAN (DEF-KAN) encoder, which captures modality-specific global features in the frequency domain, bridging spectral and spatial gaps across various datasets. Following this, a Multivariate-Gaussian-based Cross-KAN (MG-Cross-KAN) is dedicated to enhancing the robustness of cross-modality fusion by capturing modality-shared features in a distribution-based manner. Finally, to further tackle classification ambiguity under limited annotated samples, we present a visual-textual bidirectional alignment strategy, which leverages textual descriptions as supplementary semantical knowledge to clarify class feature centers. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed Two-timer-KAN achieves superior performance, outperforming the state-of-the-art methods in both accuracy and robustness.

item.title: Robust urban SLAM via resilient GNSS–IMU–LiDAR–camera–loop fusion
item.dc:creator: Minzhe Liu
Hongjuan Zhang
Zhibo Zhao
Chengzhi Hong
Haoyu Wang
Zilong Xiao
Bijun Li
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Precise positioning is critical for autonomous vehicles (AVs), which normally utilize GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System) /IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit) integrated techniques. In urban environments, the integrated system suffers from degradation of positioning accuracy because of low signal-to-noise ratio. LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging) and camera can also contribute to localization via LiDAR-IMU odometry (LIO) and Vision-IMU odometry (VIO), but they also suffer from repetitive structural scenes, illumination changes and interference from dynamic objects. Given that each type of sensor has different properties regarding the robustness in diverse urban environmental conditions, we propose a resilient GNSS–LiDAR–Vision–IMU odometry (GLVIO) and loop closure system to achieve high-precision simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM). The main contributions include: 1) The system takes a resilient fusion strategy that makes real-time decisions about how each sensor contributes to the update of each state variable via factor-graph optimization, by theoretically analyzing, modeling and propagating the uncertainty of the factors, thereby enhancing the robustness and accuracy of positioning. 2) A real-time degeneration detection algorithm is proposed for LIO, which can adaptively suppress LIO factors when degeneration occurs. 3) To enhance the reliability of visual updating, semi-dense feature extraction is performed when feature sparsity is detected. 4) To overcome the negative effects of dynamic objects on pose estimation and mapping, a 3D object-detection-based point cloud filtering module is proposed in the front-end. The effectiveness of our method is verified by comparing with SOTA methods across multiple public urban datasets. Our code is available at https://github.com/TK72/FAST-LIVOSAM as open-source software to benefit the community. Published: 2026-06-02T04:36:33+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.807 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Minzhe Liu; Hongjuan Zhang; Zhibo Zhao; Chengzhi Hong; Haoyu Wang; Zilong Xiao; Bijun Li

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.05.035

Relevance: 0.807 (must_read)

Precise positioning is critical for autonomous vehicles (AVs), which normally utilize GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System) /IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit) integrated techniques. In urban environments, the integrated system suffers from degradation of positioning accuracy because of low signal-to-noise ratio. LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging) and camera can also contribute to localization via LiDAR-IMU odometry (LIO) and Vision-IMU odometry (VIO), but they also suffer from repetitive structural scenes, illumination changes and interference from dynamic objects. Given that each type of sensor has different properties regarding the robustness in diverse urban environmental conditions, we propose a resilient GNSS–LiDAR–Vision–IMU odometry (GLVIO) and loop closure system to achieve high-precision simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM). The main contributions include: 1) The system takes a resilient fusion strategy that makes real-time decisions about how each sensor contributes to the update of each state variable via factor-graph optimization, by theoretically analyzing, modeling and propagating the uncertainty of the factors, thereby enhancing the robustness and accuracy of positioning. 2) A real-time degeneration detection algorithm is proposed for LIO, which can adaptively suppress LIO factors when degeneration occurs. 3) To enhance the reliability of visual updating, semi-dense feature extraction is performed when feature sparsity is detected. 4) To overcome the negative effects of dynamic objects on pose estimation and mapping, a 3D object-detection-based point cloud filtering module is proposed in the front-end. The effectiveness of our method is verified by comparing with SOTA methods across multiple public urban datasets. Our code is available at https://github.com/TK72/FAST-LIVOSAM as open-source software to benefit the community.

item.title: Interpretable General Image Fusion via Scalable Autoregressive Modeling
item.dc:creator: Jingwei Xin
Boneng Shi
Zhen Liang
Jun Hao
Xuehao Song
Nannan Wang
Jie Li
Xinbo Gao
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
item.description: Existing image fusion methods have developed increasingly sophisticated network architectures for exploiting modality-shared and modality-specific features. However, despite these advancements in feature extraction, most methods ultimately rely on relatively simple implicit or explicit fusion strategies, which can compromise interpretability and limit fusion accuracy. In this paper, we incorporate visual autoregressive modeling to bridge the gap between implicit feature extraction and explicit modality fusion. First, the proposed approach conducts a low-to-high resolution autoregressive objective with modality-specific features, introducing a scalable feature autoregressive mechanism. It aggregates local and global contextual dependencies while enhancing implicit cross-scale interaction. Furthermore, to promote the consistency and complementarity across modalities, we embed an explicit high-order fusion strategy within the progressive modality-specific feature extraction process. This integration facilitates a next-scale synergistic relationship between implicit learning and explicit fusion. Our High-order Feature AutoRegressive Fusion framework (HFARFusion) provides a robust and interpretable solution for general image fusion tasks, effectively balancing fusion performance and transparency through the strengths of autoregressive learning. Extensive experiments demonstrate the outstanding performance of the proposed method in several classical fusion tasks, including infrared-visible, medical, multi-focus, and multi-exposure image fusion. Our code is available at https://github.com/happysbn/HFARFusion. Published: 2026-06-02T20:09:12+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.807 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jingwei Xin; Boneng Shi; Zhen Liang; Jun Hao; Xuehao Song; Nannan Wang; Jie Li; Xinbo Gao

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3697616

Relevance: 0.807 (must_read)

Existing image fusion methods have developed increasingly sophisticated network architectures for exploiting modality-shared and modality-specific features. However, despite these advancements in feature extraction, most methods ultimately rely on relatively simple implicit or explicit fusion strategies, which can compromise interpretability and limit fusion accuracy. In this paper, we incorporate visual autoregressive modeling to bridge the gap between implicit feature extraction and explicit modality fusion. First, the proposed approach conducts a low-to-high resolution autoregressive objective with modality-specific features, introducing a scalable feature autoregressive mechanism. It aggregates local and global contextual dependencies while enhancing implicit cross-scale interaction. Furthermore, to promote the consistency and complementarity across modalities, we embed an explicit high-order fusion strategy within the progressive modality-specific feature extraction process. This integration facilitates a next-scale synergistic relationship between implicit learning and explicit fusion. Our High-order Feature AutoRegressive Fusion framework (HFARFusion) provides a robust and interpretable solution for general image fusion tasks, effectively balancing fusion performance and transparency through the strengths of autoregressive learning. Extensive experiments demonstrate the outstanding performance of the proposed method in several classical fusion tasks, including infrared-visible, medical, multi-focus, and multi-exposure image fusion. Our code is available at https://github.com/happysbn/HFARFusion.

item.title: Boosting Few-shot Continual Learning via Self-Adaptive Evolution
item.dc:creator: Ziqi Gu
Chunyan Xu
Yuanzhi Wang
Cao Han
Lei Zhang
Di Xia
Zhen Cui
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
item.description: Few-shot continual learning (FSCL) has attracted increasing attention for real-world applications, where models must continuously adapt to new classes with only a few labeled samples while retaining prior knowledge. These abilities are essential in dynamic environments where data availability is often sparse and nonstationary. However, traditional FSCL methods are largely confined to closed data spaces, which limits their generalizability when diverse and evolving distributions are involved. Inspired by the paradigm of human lifelong learning, we propose a new self-adaptive evolution framework for FSCL that enables continuous interaction with and adaptation to external environments. To exploit latent knowledge in large-scale models, we use an adaptive diffusion-based generator that not only implicitly captures the distribution of new fewshot samples but also produces more high-quality samples. To mitigate the inevitable variability in generation quality, we also use a reinforced sample selection module, comprising a generated sample explorer and a selection evaluator, which explicitly guides the retained distributions toward alignment with the large-scale models. Integrated with the continual model, these components are optimized in an iterative self-adaptive evolution framework, ensuring stable knowledge retention while improving adaptability to newly emerging classes. We validate our approach through experiments on three benchmarks, revealing its effectiveness in exploiting external distributions and achieving notable performance improvements. Published: 2026-06-02T20:09:12+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.804 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ziqi Gu; Chunyan Xu; Yuanzhi Wang; Cao Han; Lei Zhang; Di Xia; Zhen Cui

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3697649

Relevance: 0.804 (must_read)

Few-shot continual learning (FSCL) has attracted increasing attention for real-world applications, where models must continuously adapt to new classes with only a few labeled samples while retaining prior knowledge. These abilities are essential in dynamic environments where data availability is often sparse and nonstationary. However, traditional FSCL methods are largely confined to closed data spaces, which limits their generalizability when diverse and evolving distributions are involved. Inspired by the paradigm of human lifelong learning, we propose a new self-adaptive evolution framework for FSCL that enables continuous interaction with and adaptation to external environments. To exploit latent knowledge in large-scale models, we use an adaptive diffusion-based generator that not only implicitly captures the distribution of new fewshot samples but also produces more high-quality samples. To mitigate the inevitable variability in generation quality, we also use a reinforced sample selection module, comprising a generated sample explorer and a selection evaluator, which explicitly guides the retained distributions toward alignment with the large-scale models. Integrated with the continual model, these components are optimized in an iterative self-adaptive evolution framework, ensuring stable knowledge retention while improving adaptability to newly emerging classes. We validate our approach through experiments on three benchmarks, revealing its effectiveness in exploiting external distributions and achieving notable performance improvements.

item.title: Semantic-Decoupled and Knowledge-Shared Probabilistic Mapping Network for Multi-Grained Cross-Modal Retrieval
item.dc:creator: Wenrui Li
Yeyu Chai
Liang-Jian Deng
Ruiqin Xiong
Xiaopeng Fan
Yonghong Tian
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
item.description: Cross-modal retrieval is essential for exploring semantic correlations between multimodal data. However, existing approaches face challenges in resolving semantic ambiguity and transferring knowledge with sparse sample generalization. To address these challenges, we propose a new Semantic-Decoupled and Knowledge-Shared Probabilistic Mapping Network (SKPMN). Specifically, the Semantic Decoupling and Distinction (SDD) module decomposes complex word-region relationships into relevance-driven representations. The Deep Probability Mapping (DPM) module introduces a paradigm shift by mapping multimodal features into probabilistic distributions, capturing the semantic similarities and the potential uncertainties that define sparse or ambiguous relationships. By combining the Attention Probabilistic Mapping (APM) module, the model can effectively transfer knowledge across similar samples while emphasizing critical distinctions, significantly enhancing generalization to sparse and ambiguous samples. Finally, the multi-grained alignment strategy establishes a novel integration of fine-grained patch-to-word alignment and coarse-grained global alignment. Experimental results show that SKPMN achieves superior retrieval accuracy across major benchmark datasets. Furthermore, we implement a channel resource allocation technique that allocates more transmission resources to semantically significant information. In resource-constrained environments, our approach leverages Joint Source-Channel Coding (JSCC) to enhance the efficiency of visual feature transmission. Published: 2026-06-02T20:09:12+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.802 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Wenrui Li; Yeyu Chai; Liang-Jian Deng; Ruiqin Xiong; Xiaopeng Fan; Yonghong Tian

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3697617

Relevance: 0.802 (must_read)

Cross-modal retrieval is essential for exploring semantic correlations between multimodal data. However, existing approaches face challenges in resolving semantic ambiguity and transferring knowledge with sparse sample generalization. To address these challenges, we propose a new Semantic-Decoupled and Knowledge-Shared Probabilistic Mapping Network (SKPMN). Specifically, the Semantic Decoupling and Distinction (SDD) module decomposes complex word-region relationships into relevance-driven representations. The Deep Probability Mapping (DPM) module introduces a paradigm shift by mapping multimodal features into probabilistic distributions, capturing the semantic similarities and the potential uncertainties that define sparse or ambiguous relationships. By combining the Attention Probabilistic Mapping (APM) module, the model can effectively transfer knowledge across similar samples while emphasizing critical distinctions, significantly enhancing generalization to sparse and ambiguous samples. Finally, the multi-grained alignment strategy establishes a novel integration of fine-grained patch-to-word alignment and coarse-grained global alignment. Experimental results show that SKPMN achieves superior retrieval accuracy across major benchmark datasets. Furthermore, we implement a channel resource allocation technique that allocates more transmission resources to semantically significant information. In resource-constrained environments, our approach leverages Joint Source-Channel Coding (JSCC) to enhance the efficiency of visual feature transmission.

item.title: Robust SAR Ship Detection with Texture Perception Convolution and Region Balanced Sampler
item.dc:creator: Hangyu Cao
Zhao Chen
item.prism:publicationName: Remote Sensing
item.description: High-resolution synthetic aperture radar (SAR) ship detection plays a pivotal role in maritime surveillance and ocean monitoring. However, it remains challenging in practice because of the single-channel imaging modality, severe multiplicative speckle noise, and the pronounced scale imbalance in which sparse large vessels are easily under-optimized compared to the dominant small and medium instances. In this paper, we propose a Texture Perception Convolution Network (TPCNet), a practical and reproducible detection framework to improve feature extraction robustness and high-IoU localization under a unified strict-COCO evaluation protocol. TPCNet begins with a lightweight texture perception convolution (TPConv) that augments the raw SAR intensity with a local fluctuation cue to stabilize early feature representations for SAR images affected by strong multiplicative speckle noise (speckle-rich imagery). To address scale skew during training without modifying dataset splits, a region-balanced sampler (RBS) is introduced to increase the sampling probability of images, thereby improving the effective exposure of informative large-target structures. A background similarity augmentation (BSA) is proposed to enrich medium and large instances while reducing unrealistic boundary artifacts via compatible background selection and soft blending. Beyond component-level designs, the high-IoU localization is highly sensitive to geometric perturbations. Accordingly, TPCNet adopts a two-stage localization-oriented training strategy that first learns robust multi-scale representations and then refines box regression by tightening translation and scale ranges during fine-tuning. Under strict-COCO settings, TPCNet achieves SOTA performance with an AP of 71.20% on HRSID and an AP of 72.7% on SSDD. Comprehensive ablation studies demonstrate that TPConv, RBS, BSA, and the proposed finetuning strategy contribute complementary gains, providing a transparent baseline and a strong recipe for future SAR ship detection research. Published: 2026-06-02T10:14:57+00:00 Venue: Remote Sensing Score: 0.800 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Hangyu Cao; Zhao Chen

Journal: Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.3390/rs18111807

Relevance: 0.800 (must_read)

High-resolution synthetic aperture radar (SAR) ship detection plays a pivotal role in maritime surveillance and ocean monitoring. However, it remains challenging in practice because of the single-channel imaging modality, severe multiplicative speckle noise, and the pronounced scale imbalance in which sparse large vessels are easily under-optimized compared to the dominant small and medium instances. In this paper, we propose a Texture Perception Convolution Network (TPCNet), a practical and reproducible detection framework to improve feature extraction robustness and high-IoU localization under a unified strict-COCO evaluation protocol. TPCNet begins with a lightweight texture perception convolution (TPConv) that augments the raw SAR intensity with a local fluctuation cue to stabilize early feature representations for SAR images affected by strong multiplicative speckle noise (speckle-rich imagery). To address scale skew during training without modifying dataset splits, a region-balanced sampler (RBS) is introduced to increase the sampling probability of images, thereby improving the effective exposure of informative large-target structures. A background similarity augmentation (BSA) is proposed to enrich medium and large instances while reducing unrealistic boundary artifacts via compatible background selection and soft blending. Beyond component-level designs, the high-IoU localization is highly sensitive to geometric perturbations. Accordingly, TPCNet adopts a two-stage localization-oriented training strategy that first learns robust multi-scale representations and then refines box regression by tightening translation and scale ranges during fine-tuning. Under strict-COCO settings, TPCNet achieves SOTA performance with an AP of 71.20% on HRSID and an AP of 72.7% on SSDD. Comprehensive ablation studies demonstrate that TPConv, RBS, BSA, and the proposed finetuning strategy contribute complementary gains, providing a transparent baseline and a strong recipe for future SAR ship detection research.

item.title: Domain Adaptation with a Single Vision-Language Embedding
item.dc:creator: Mohammad Fahes
Tuan-Hung Vu
Andrei Bursuc
Patrick Pérez
Raoul de Charette
item.prism:publicationName: International Journal of Computer Vision
item.description: Domain adaptation has been extensively investigated in computer vision but still requires access to target data at the training time, which might be difficult to obtain in real-world autonomous driving scenarios, especially under rare or adverse conditions. In this paper, we present a new framework for domain adaptation relying on a single Vision-Language (VL) latent embedding instead of full target data. First, leveraging a contrastive language-image pre-training model (CLIP), we propose prompt/photo-driven instance normalization (PIN). PIN is a feature augmentation method that mines multiple visual styles using a single target VL latent embedding, by optimizing affine transformations of low-level source features. The VL embedding can come from a language prompt describing the target domain, a partially optimized language prompt, or a single unlabeled target image. Second, we show that these mined styles (i.e., augmentations) can be used for zero-shot (i.e., target-free) and one-shot unsupervised domain adaptation. Experiments on semantic segmentation in real-world driving datasets, including Cityscapes and ACDC (adverse conditions), demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method, which outperforms relevant baselines in the practical zero-shot and one-shot settings. Published: 2026-06-03T16:45:18+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Computer Vision Score: 0.799 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Mohammad Fahes; Tuan-Hung Vu; Andrei Bursuc; Patrick Pérez; Raoul de Charette

Journal: International Journal of Computer Vision

DOI: 10.1007/s11263-026-02875-3

Relevance: 0.799 (must_read)

Domain adaptation has been extensively investigated in computer vision but still requires access to target data at the training time, which might be difficult to obtain in real-world autonomous driving scenarios, especially under rare or adverse conditions. In this paper, we present a new framework for domain adaptation relying on a single Vision-Language (VL) latent embedding instead of full target data. First, leveraging a contrastive language-image pre-training model (CLIP), we propose prompt/photo-driven instance normalization (PIN). PIN is a feature augmentation method that mines multiple visual styles using a single target VL latent embedding, by optimizing affine transformations of low-level source features. The VL embedding can come from a language prompt describing the target domain, a partially optimized language prompt, or a single unlabeled target image. Second, we show that these mined styles (i.e., augmentations) can be used for zero-shot (i.e., target-free) and one-shot unsupervised domain adaptation. Experiments on semantic segmentation in real-world driving datasets, including Cityscapes and ACDC (adverse conditions), demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method, which outperforms relevant baselines in the practical zero-shot and one-shot settings.

item.title: Learning Instance-Level Knowledge with Image-Level Supervision for Open-Vocabulary Object Detection in Remote Sensing Images
item.dc:creator: Yan Li
Yunpeng Bai
Jiaman Ma
Amirkhan Temirbayev
Ying Li
Changjing Shang
Qiang Shen
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Open-Vocabulary Object Detection (OVOD) has shown significant potential in real-world remote sensing applications, thanks to its adaptability to open category spaces. Existing OVOD methods mainly rely on pretrained Vision–Language Models (VLMs) to recognize unknown categories. However, VLMs are usually trained on image–text pairs. Whilst possessing strong global semantic modeling capabilities, they struggle to accurately capture the relationship between object instances and their precise locations. To address this problem, the paper presents LILK, which Learns Instance-Level Knowledge from image-level data through weak supervision. Specifically, an image-level weakly supervised knowledge injection module is first created, which represents instance-level semantic information by introducing global classification queries, while aligning image-level supervision with instance-level supervision through a distinct query promotion strategy. Secondly, a quality-aware pseudo-label rectification module is developed, which filters candidate boxes using dual thresholds based on detector confidence and semantic consistency, while incorporating a SAM2-based relocation mechanism to enhance spatial localization accuracy of pseudo labels. Finally, an image-guided query enhancement module is introduced to guide and reinforce detection queries using instance-level image features, providing the detector with stable and modality-consistent visual references. Extensive experimental results on three commonly used remote sensing object detection datasets, DIOR, DOTA, and NWPU VHR-10, demonstrate the efficacy of LILK in performing OVOD tasks. Published: 2026-06-03T19:40:37+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.798 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yan Li; Yunpeng Bai; Jiaman Ma; Amirkhan Temirbayev; Ying Li; Changjing Shang; Qiang Shen

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3699883

Relevance: 0.798 (must_read)

Open-Vocabulary Object Detection (OVOD) has shown significant potential in real-world remote sensing applications, thanks to its adaptability to open category spaces. Existing OVOD methods mainly rely on pretrained Vision–Language Models (VLMs) to recognize unknown categories. However, VLMs are usually trained on image–text pairs. Whilst possessing strong global semantic modeling capabilities, they struggle to accurately capture the relationship between object instances and their precise locations. To address this problem, the paper presents LILK, which Learns Instance-Level Knowledge from image-level data through weak supervision. Specifically, an image-level weakly supervised knowledge injection module is first created, which represents instance-level semantic information by introducing global classification queries, while aligning image-level supervision with instance-level supervision through a distinct query promotion strategy. Secondly, a quality-aware pseudo-label rectification module is developed, which filters candidate boxes using dual thresholds based on detector confidence and semantic consistency, while incorporating a SAM2-based relocation mechanism to enhance spatial localization accuracy of pseudo labels. Finally, an image-guided query enhancement module is introduced to guide and reinforce detection queries using instance-level image features, providing the detector with stable and modality-consistent visual references. Extensive experimental results on three commonly used remote sensing object detection datasets, DIOR, DOTA, and NWPU VHR-10, demonstrate the efficacy of LILK in performing OVOD tasks.

item.title: SAINet: Scale-aware Adaptive Interactive Semantic Segmentation Network for Remote Sensing Scenarios
item.dc:creator: Shuaiqi Liu
Zhonghao Deng
Jiahui Cao
Bing Li
Yudong Zhang
Hui Wang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: In the semantic segmentation of remote sensing images (RSIs), heterogeneous modalities often exhibit distribution discrepancies and noise interference. How to effectively mitigate modality gaps and reduce noise while fully exploiting the complementary information across multimodal data remains a challenging problem. To tackle this issue, a Scale-aware Adaptive Interactive Network (SAINet) was proposed for the semantic segmentation of remote sensing scenes. First, SAINet employs an Adaptive Feature Interaction (AFI) module to align features from optical RSIs and Digital Surface Models (DSM) at both spatial and channel levels, thereby alleviating cross-modal inconsistency and enhancing the complementary utilization of multimodal information. Second, by introducing hierarchical contextual semantic representations, SAINet constructs a Multi-Scale Context Aggregation (MSCA) module, which strengthens the model’s capability to perceive objects at different scales as well as complex land-cover distributions. Furthermore, a Symmetric Dilated Spatial Mixer (SDSM) is designed to effectively balance local and global dependency modeling, while maintaining favorable computational efficiency and stability when processing high-resolution inputs. Finally, comprehensive experiments are performed on two widely used segmentation datasets, namely Vaihingen and Potsdam, and the obtained results indicate that SAINet delivers superior segmentation performance. Published: 2026-06-02T20:06:09+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.798 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Shuaiqi Liu; Zhonghao Deng; Jiahui Cao; Bing Li; Yudong Zhang; Hui Wang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3699229

Relevance: 0.798 (must_read)

In the semantic segmentation of remote sensing images (RSIs), heterogeneous modalities often exhibit distribution discrepancies and noise interference. How to effectively mitigate modality gaps and reduce noise while fully exploiting the complementary information across multimodal data remains a challenging problem. To tackle this issue, a Scale-aware Adaptive Interactive Network (SAINet) was proposed for the semantic segmentation of remote sensing scenes. First, SAINet employs an Adaptive Feature Interaction (AFI) module to align features from optical RSIs and Digital Surface Models (DSM) at both spatial and channel levels, thereby alleviating cross-modal inconsistency and enhancing the complementary utilization of multimodal information. Second, by introducing hierarchical contextual semantic representations, SAINet constructs a Multi-Scale Context Aggregation (MSCA) module, which strengthens the model’s capability to perceive objects at different scales as well as complex land-cover distributions. Furthermore, a Symmetric Dilated Spatial Mixer (SDSM) is designed to effectively balance local and global dependency modeling, while maintaining favorable computational efficiency and stability when processing high-resolution inputs. Finally, comprehensive experiments are performed on two widely used segmentation datasets, namely Vaihingen and Potsdam, and the obtained results indicate that SAINet delivers superior segmentation performance.

item.title: Listen2Track: Plug-and-Play Audio-Visual Matching for Generic Multi-Object Tracking
item.dc:creator: Yeqiang Liu
Weiran Li
Yijie Wei
Mina Han
Fukuan Wang
Yining Fan
Wenxu Wang
Zhenbo Li
item.prism:publicationName: Information Fusion
item.description: Audio-visual multi-object tracking (AV-MOT) aims to track sound-producing objects in video sequences by leveraging both acoustic and visual information. This paradigm enables trackers to identify and follow targets based on their audio-visual correspondences, distinguishing sounding objects from silent ones through acoustic signatures. However, existing audio-visual methods require specialized architectures that are difficult to integrate with established tracking frameworks. We propose Listen2Track , an audio-visual augmentation framework that equips existing MOT systems with acoustic perception while keeping the base detector and tracker frozen. In our setting, only the proposed lightweight audio-visual modules are trained offline, and they are attached to existing tracking pipelines through detection filtering and association-score enhancement. Our approach consists of two modules: Object-Level Audio-Visual contrastive learning (OLAV) learns correspondences between objects and sounds through bidirectional InfoNCE, while Audio-Visual Association enhances temporal matching via FiLM-based audio-guided modulation of visual features with track-level audio memory. Both modules are integrated by enhancing the association scores used in existing trackers, rather than retraining the original tracking architecture. Experiments demonstrate consistent improvements across diverse tracking architectures, with significant gains in identity preservation and tracking accuracy. Listen2Track provides a practical solution for enabling existing MOT systems to track sound-producing objects through acoustic perception. Published: 2026-06-02T16:34:05+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.798 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yeqiang Liu; Weiran Li; Yijie Wei; Mina Han; Fukuan Wang; Yining Fan; Wenxu Wang; Zhenbo Li

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104519

Relevance: 0.798 (must_read)

Audio-visual multi-object tracking (AV-MOT) aims to track sound-producing objects in video sequences by leveraging both acoustic and visual information. This paradigm enables trackers to identify and follow targets based on their audio-visual correspondences, distinguishing sounding objects from silent ones through acoustic signatures. However, existing audio-visual methods require specialized architectures that are difficult to integrate with established tracking frameworks. We propose Listen2Track , an audio-visual augmentation framework that equips existing MOT systems with acoustic perception while keeping the base detector and tracker frozen. In our setting, only the proposed lightweight audio-visual modules are trained offline, and they are attached to existing tracking pipelines through detection filtering and association-score enhancement. Our approach consists of two modules: Object-Level Audio-Visual contrastive learning (OLAV) learns correspondences between objects and sounds through bidirectional InfoNCE, while Audio-Visual Association enhances temporal matching via FiLM-based audio-guided modulation of visual features with track-level audio memory. Both modules are integrated by enhancing the association scores used in existing trackers, rather than retraining the original tracking architecture. Experiments demonstrate consistent improvements across diverse tracking architectures, with significant gains in identity preservation and tracking accuracy. Listen2Track provides a practical solution for enabling existing MOT systems to track sound-producing objects through acoustic perception.

item.title: TORA: Parameter-Efficient Token-driven Residual Adaptation of Remote Sensing Foundation Models for Multi-Scale Agricultural Vision
item.dc:creator: Haocheng Li
Juepeng Zheng
Shuangxi Miao
Kaiqi Du
Guilong Xiao
Zhiwei Zhang
Lingyuan Zhao
Haohuan Fu
Jianxi Huang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Precision agriculture increasingly relies on robust visual understanding of crops and fields. However, agricultural scenes remain challenging due to large appearance variability, background clutter, and fine-grained multi-scale structures. Although remote sensing foundation models (RSFMs) have demonstrated strong generalization in generic Earth observation tasks, adapting them effectively to domain-specific agricultural applications is still non-trivial. Existing parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) strategies often struggle to balance semantic adaptability across diverse crop phenotypes and domain shifts with spatial fidelity in preserving fine-grained structures and boundaries, which limits their effectiveness on dense prediction tasks. To this end, we propose TORA (Token-driven Residual Adaptation), an efficient adaptation paradigm tailored to multi-scale agricultural vision. TORA augments frozen RSFMs with hierarchical learnable semantic prototypes to explicitly model domain-specific semantic patterns and to interact with intermediate representations through token-wise attention. Meanwhile, we design an instance-aware residual path to inject adaptation signals into backbone features, thereby strengthening domain alignment while effectively mitigating the degradation of spatial details. Furthermore, we establish a comprehensive evaluation benchmark spanning three representative tasks—semantic segmentation, object detection, and image classification—across nine datasets. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TORA trains only approximately 1.5%–3.8% of the backbone parameters, substantially fewer than full fine-tuning, yet achieves performance that is competitive with or superior to strong PEFT baselines and approaches full fine-tuning on multiple datasets. Published: 2026-06-02T20:06:09+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.797 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Haocheng Li; Juepeng Zheng; Shuangxi Miao; Kaiqi Du; Guilong Xiao; Zhiwei Zhang; Lingyuan Zhao; Haohuan Fu; Jianxi Huang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3699401

Relevance: 0.797 (must_read)

Precision agriculture increasingly relies on robust visual understanding of crops and fields. However, agricultural scenes remain challenging due to large appearance variability, background clutter, and fine-grained multi-scale structures. Although remote sensing foundation models (RSFMs) have demonstrated strong generalization in generic Earth observation tasks, adapting them effectively to domain-specific agricultural applications is still non-trivial. Existing parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) strategies often struggle to balance semantic adaptability across diverse crop phenotypes and domain shifts with spatial fidelity in preserving fine-grained structures and boundaries, which limits their effectiveness on dense prediction tasks. To this end, we propose TORA (Token-driven Residual Adaptation), an efficient adaptation paradigm tailored to multi-scale agricultural vision. TORA augments frozen RSFMs with hierarchical learnable semantic prototypes to explicitly model domain-specific semantic patterns and to interact with intermediate representations through token-wise attention. Meanwhile, we design an instance-aware residual path to inject adaptation signals into backbone features, thereby strengthening domain alignment while effectively mitigating the degradation of spatial details. Furthermore, we establish a comprehensive evaluation benchmark spanning three representative tasks—semantic segmentation, object detection, and image classification—across nine datasets. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TORA trains only approximately 1.5%–3.8% of the backbone parameters, substantially fewer than full fine-tuning, yet achieves performance that is competitive with or superior to strong PEFT baselines and approaches full fine-tuning on multiple datasets.

item.title: FGDNet: Frequency-domain guided degradation-aware network for object detection in adverse weather
item.dc:creator: Yingjun Wang
Shuxin Zhang
Xiaopeng Yang
Deguang Li
Zheng Liang
Wenyi Zhao
Weidong Zhang
item.prism:publicationName: Information Sciences
item.description: Object detection in adverse environments is challenging due to spectral distortions and spatial aliasing caused by physical degradations such as haze and low illumination, which often lead to missed detections and inaccurate localization. Existing methods mainly rely on spatial-domain representations, limiting their ability to effectively disentangle target structures from environmental noise in highly mixed signals. To address this issue, we propose a frequency-domain guided degradation-aware network (FGDNet) for robust object detection by jointly exploiting frequency-domain global cues and spatial geometric priors. Specifically, a frequency-selective interaction module (FSIM) is introduced to explicitly model frequency correlations, enabling effective degradation characterization and enhanced perception of global spectral information. Furthermore, a frequency-guided feature enhancement module (FGFE) performs cross-domain feature calibration via learnable frequency modulation, facilitating joint enhancement of local textures and global structural representations. In addition, an asymmetric geometric prior is incorporated to adaptively reweight fused features, strengthening weak-response targets while suppressing complex background interference. Extensive experiments on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that FGDNet consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving 78.5% mAP 50 on the RTTS dataset, while maintaining a lightweight design with only 13M parameters, enabling efficient deployment in resource-constrained scenarios. The source code will be publicly available at: https://github.com/zsx0506/FGDNet Published: 2026-06-03T23:52:29+00:00 Venue: Information Sciences Score: 0.795 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yingjun Wang; Shuxin Zhang; Xiaopeng Yang; Deguang Li; Zheng Liang; Wenyi Zhao; Weidong Zhang

Journal: Information Sciences

DOI: 10.1016/j.ins.2026.123743

Relevance: 0.795 (must_read)

Object detection in adverse environments is challenging due to spectral distortions and spatial aliasing caused by physical degradations such as haze and low illumination, which often lead to missed detections and inaccurate localization. Existing methods mainly rely on spatial-domain representations, limiting their ability to effectively disentangle target structures from environmental noise in highly mixed signals. To address this issue, we propose a frequency-domain guided degradation-aware network (FGDNet) for robust object detection by jointly exploiting frequency-domain global cues and spatial geometric priors. Specifically, a frequency-selective interaction module (FSIM) is introduced to explicitly model frequency correlations, enabling effective degradation characterization and enhanced perception of global spectral information. Furthermore, a frequency-guided feature enhancement module (FGFE) performs cross-domain feature calibration via learnable frequency modulation, facilitating joint enhancement of local textures and global structural representations. In addition, an asymmetric geometric prior is incorporated to adaptively reweight fused features, strengthening weak-response targets while suppressing complex background interference. Extensive experiments on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that FGDNet consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving 78.5% mAP 50 on the RTTS dataset, while maintaining a lightweight design with only 13M parameters, enabling efficient deployment in resource-constrained scenarios. The source code will be publicly available at: https://github.com/zsx0506/FGDNet

item.title: Phy-OSShip-MS Dataset: A Paired Optical-SAR Ship Imagery Generation Method Via Hierarchical Physics-Guided Style Transfer
item.dc:creator: Jing Li
Yan Zhang
Zhuanze Yang
Chao Song
Ranhui Yuan
Feifan Ma
Jintao Chen
Zhongxing Jiao
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
item.description: High-quality spatiotemporally paired optical and Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) datasets are fundamental for cross-modal ship detection tasks. However, due to distinct imaging mechanisms, difficulties in acquiring spatiotemporal consistency, and data privacy concerns, such public datasets are extremely scarce. Current cross-modal SAR image synthesis methods often struggle to balance physical consistency and perceptual fidelity, resulting in images with scattering distributions that violate imaging mechanisms and speckle statistics that deviate from real data. To address these challenges, this article proposes a Hierarchical Physics-Guided Style Transfer framework for Optical-to-SAR image generation. In this architecture, style transfer serves as an underlying generative backbone to ensure perceptual fidelity, while a novel “Radiometric-Geometric-Fusion” three-level physical guidance strategy is introduced to explicitly constrain the generation process. First, a scene-universal category-level radiometric prior is constructed to establish physical intensity references for different categories. Second, phase congruency features are introduced to compensate for the deficiency of radiometric priors in fine structural depiction, precisely preserving the critical geometric details of strong scattering targets like ships. Finally, a synergistic fusion mechanism based on physical attribute intersection is established to apply directional gain to strong scattering targets by coupling material attributes with geometric saliency, thereby ensuring intensity-level physical consistency. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing image-to-image translation models in terms of texture details and scattering characteristics. Consequently, we establish a Physics-guided Optical-SAR Ship Imagery dataset with Multi-Source attributes (Phy-OSShip-MS), providing target-level synthetically paired samples as an effective generative data augmentation strategy... Published: 2026-06-02T20:06:36+00:00 Venue: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing Score: 0.793 (must_read)
