High-quality spatiotemporally paired optical and Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) datasets are fundamental for cross-modal ship detection tasks. However, due to distinct imaging mechanisms, difficulties in acquiring spatiotemporal consistency, and data privacy concerns, such public datasets are extremely scarce. Current cross-modal SAR image synthesis methods often struggle to balance physical consistency and perceptual fidelity, resulting in images with scattering distributions that violate imaging mechanisms and speckle statistics that deviate from real data. To address these challenges, this article proposes a Hierarchical Physics-Guided Style Transfer framework for Optical-to-SAR image generation. In this architecture, style transfer serves as an underlying generative backbone to ensure perceptual fidelity, while a novel “Radiometric-Geometric-Fusion” three-level physical guidance strategy is introduced to explicitly constrain the generation process. First, a scene-universal category-level radiometric prior is constructed to establish physical intensity references for different categories. Second, phase congruency features are introduced to compensate for the deficiency of radiometric priors in fine structural depiction, precisely preserving the critical geometric details of strong scattering targets like ships. Finally, a synergistic fusion mechanism based on physical attribute intersection is established to apply directional gain to strong scattering targets by coupling material attributes with geometric saliency, thereby ensuring intensity-level physical consistency. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing image-to-image translation models in terms of texture details and scattering characteristics. Consequently, we establish a Physics-guided Optical-SAR Ship Imagery dataset with Multi-Source attributes (Phy-OSShip-MS), providing target-level synthetically paired samples as an effective generative data augmentation strategy...

item.title: DDC-Det: A Dual-Domain Collaborative Framework for Degraded Aerial Object Detection via Contextual Compensation and Physical-Prior Restoration
item.dc:creator: Liming Zhou
Shiqi Wang
Shanjiu Wang
Shumin Wang
Xiaolong Ji
Tengyu Yin
Yang Liu
item.prism:publicationName: Expert Systems with Applications
item.description: Aerial object detection faces a significant performance bottleneck in real-world deployment due to severe physical degradations, such as motion blur and defocus artifacts caused by high-frequency vibrations and high-speed maneuvers of UAV platforms. These degradations compromise the high-frequency structural features of small objects, leading to catastrophic feature attenuation. While current methods primarily rely on black-box optimization, they frequently neglect the underlying physical mechanisms of image formation, resulting in suboptimal robustness and computational redundancy. This paper proposes DDC-Det, a novel dual-domain collaborative framework that unifies macro-level contextual compensation with micro-level physical-prior-guided restoration. Specifically, a Contextual Compensation Backbone (CC-Backbone) is developed, utilizing a Stage-Adaptive Dynamic Mixer (SADM) to dynamically adjust receptive fields and mitigate semantic ambiguity through global contextual inference. To address localized structural damage, a physical-knowledge-aware feature restoration neck is designed. Within this neck, the Gabor-guided Omnidirectional Aggregation (GODA) module injects optical priors via parameterized dynamic Gabor filters to reconstruct high-frequency edges, while the Dynamic Directional Routing (DDR) module employs a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) mechanism to simulate adaptive inverse filtering for precise directional blur correction. Extensive evaluations on the VisDrone and UAVDT datasets demonstrate that the DDC-Det achieves superior detection performance with mAP 0.5 scores of 50.5% and 40.8%, respectively, establishing highly competitive benchmarks in aerial imagery. Furthermore, with only 14.3M parameters, DDC-Det significantly outperforms advanced models based on Transformers on the CODrone-Blur benchmark, strongly demonstrating that the dual domain synergy between physical priors and macro context is crucial for enhancing the intrinsic robustness of the model. Published: 2026-06-02T16:30:17+00:00 Venue: Expert Systems with Applications Score: 0.792 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Liming Zhou; Shiqi Wang; Shanjiu Wang; Shumin Wang; Xiaolong Ji; Tengyu Yin; Yang Liu

Journal: Expert Systems with Applications

DOI: 10.1016/j.eswa.2026.133096

Relevance: 0.792 (must_read)

Aerial object detection faces a significant performance bottleneck in real-world deployment due to severe physical degradations, such as motion blur and defocus artifacts caused by high-frequency vibrations and high-speed maneuvers of UAV platforms. These degradations compromise the high-frequency structural features of small objects, leading to catastrophic feature attenuation. While current methods primarily rely on black-box optimization, they frequently neglect the underlying physical mechanisms of image formation, resulting in suboptimal robustness and computational redundancy. This paper proposes DDC-Det, a novel dual-domain collaborative framework that unifies macro-level contextual compensation with micro-level physical-prior-guided restoration. Specifically, a Contextual Compensation Backbone (CC-Backbone) is developed, utilizing a Stage-Adaptive Dynamic Mixer (SADM) to dynamically adjust receptive fields and mitigate semantic ambiguity through global contextual inference. To address localized structural damage, a physical-knowledge-aware feature restoration neck is designed. Within this neck, the Gabor-guided Omnidirectional Aggregation (GODA) module injects optical priors via parameterized dynamic Gabor filters to reconstruct high-frequency edges, while the Dynamic Directional Routing (DDR) module employs a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) mechanism to simulate adaptive inverse filtering for precise directional blur correction. Extensive evaluations on the VisDrone and UAVDT datasets demonstrate that the DDC-Det achieves superior detection performance with mAP 0.5 scores of 50.5% and 40.8%, respectively, establishing highly competitive benchmarks in aerial imagery. Furthermore, with only 14.3M parameters, DDC-Det significantly outperforms advanced models based on Transformers on the CODrone-Blur benchmark, strongly demonstrating that the dual domain synergy between physical priors and macro context is crucial for enhancing the intrinsic robustness of the model.

item.title: Terrain-Adaptive LiDAR-IMU SLAM with Multi-Feature fusion for complex underground environments
item.dc:creator: Aiqiang Ma
Xiaohu Lin
Wanqiang Yao
Bolin Ma
Yinan Gao
Xin Yang
Tao Yan
Xiang Wang
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Mobile robots empowered by Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) technology are becoming a key enabler of next-generation underground intelligence, providing essential support for intelligent management and safety monitoring of underground infrastructure. However, existing LiDAR SLAM methods overly rely on geometric features while neglecting point cloud intensity information and ground structural constraints, leading to reduced front-end robustness and limited back-end fusion performance. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a terrain-adaptive LiDAR-IMU SLAM framework that systematically integrates intensity-feature enhancement with Riemannian-manifold-based ground constraints. First, an adaptive feature-entropy enhancement method based on a spherical-coordinate intensity field is designed. A spherical intensity model with a six-grid averaging scheme is employed to extract reflection characteristics, significantly improving geometric matching accuracy and pose estimation robustness in low-texture underground environments. Second, a terrain-adaptation mechanism on Riemannian manifolds is designed, where a slope-dynamics model with normal-vector geodesic distances captures topographic variations. An exponential forgetting-weighted strategy within a sliding window further enables smooth adaptation to gradual-to-abrupt slope changes. Finally, a multi-feature fusion optimization module is developed, which dynamically balances geometric and intensity features within a sliding-window factor graph. This module jointly refines loop closure, LiDAR keyframe, and IMU pre-integration constraints. Extensive experiments conducted in representative underground environments, including subway tunnels, coal mine roadways, and underground parking lots facilities, demonstrate that the proposed method reduces vertical drift error by 67.78% and achieves a minimum absolute pose error of 0.254 m. These results confirm significant improvements in accuracy and robustness, providing a reliable technical foundation for intelligent underground inspection and infrastructure monitoring. Published: 2026-06-02T04:36:33+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.792 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Aiqiang Ma; Xiaohu Lin; Wanqiang Yao; Bolin Ma; Yinan Gao; Xin Yang; Tao Yan; Xiang Wang

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.05.045

Relevance: 0.792 (must_read)

Mobile robots empowered by Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) technology are becoming a key enabler of next-generation underground intelligence, providing essential support for intelligent management and safety monitoring of underground infrastructure. However, existing LiDAR SLAM methods overly rely on geometric features while neglecting point cloud intensity information and ground structural constraints, leading to reduced front-end robustness and limited back-end fusion performance. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a terrain-adaptive LiDAR-IMU SLAM framework that systematically integrates intensity-feature enhancement with Riemannian-manifold-based ground constraints. First, an adaptive feature-entropy enhancement method based on a spherical-coordinate intensity field is designed. A spherical intensity model with a six-grid averaging scheme is employed to extract reflection characteristics, significantly improving geometric matching accuracy and pose estimation robustness in low-texture underground environments. Second, a terrain-adaptation mechanism on Riemannian manifolds is designed, where a slope-dynamics model with normal-vector geodesic distances captures topographic variations. An exponential forgetting-weighted strategy within a sliding window further enables smooth adaptation to gradual-to-abrupt slope changes. Finally, a multi-feature fusion optimization module is developed, which dynamically balances geometric and intensity features within a sliding-window factor graph. This module jointly refines loop closure, LiDAR keyframe, and IMU pre-integration constraints. Extensive experiments conducted in representative underground environments, including subway tunnels, coal mine roadways, and underground parking lots facilities, demonstrate that the proposed method reduces vertical drift error by 67.78% and achieves a minimum absolute pose error of 0.254 m. These results confirm significant improvements in accuracy and robustness, providing a reliable technical foundation for intelligent underground inspection and infrastructure monitoring.

item.title: Sub-Look Decomposition: Analysis and Application for Ship Classification in TOPSAR Data
item.dc:creator: Al Adil Al Hinai
Raffaella Guida
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
item.description: This study investigates the amplitude artefacts induced by sub-look decomposition (SD) in terrain observation by progressive scans synthetic aperture radar (TOPSAR) imagery and their implications for ship classification. The work presents a dual contribution: first, a theoretical framework is developed to derive the mechanism by which TOPSAR's inherent phase modulation is transformed into periodic amplitude artefacts during the SD process. Second, a novel, lightweight convolutional neural network (CNN), LeNet-4SD, is proposed to effectively leverage SD data. The model's architecture is distinguished by a multi-input design that processes azimuth sub-looks (AZS), range sub-looks (RGS), and the single look complex (SLC) amplitude image, through parallel branches fused by a spectrum-based attention mechanism. An evaluation on the OpenSARShip dataset demonstrates that LeNet-4SD achieves classification performance comparable to significantly larger benchmark models while being over two orders of magnitude more computationally efficient. To interpret the model's behaviour, Shapley additive explanations (SHAP) was used to quantify the contribution of each data source. The analysis reveals distinct, class-specific dependencies, highlighting the model's reliance on sub-look diversity for container ships and on the high-resolution SLC for tankers. These findings underscore the importance of accounting for processing-induced artefacts and establish that custom, lightweight, multi-input models can be a more effective and efficient strategy for SAR applications than fine-tuning large, pre-trained networks. Published: 2026-06-03T19:41:33+00:00 Venue: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing Score: 0.792 (must_read)

Journal: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/jstars.2026.3699797

Relevance: 0.792 (must_read)

This study investigates the amplitude artefacts induced by sub-look decomposition (SD) in terrain observation by progressive scans synthetic aperture radar (TOPSAR) imagery and their implications for ship classification. The work presents a dual contribution: first, a theoretical framework is developed to derive the mechanism by which TOPSAR's inherent phase modulation is transformed into periodic amplitude artefacts during the SD process. Second, a novel, lightweight convolutional neural network (CNN), LeNet-4SD, is proposed to effectively leverage SD data. The model's architecture is distinguished by a multi-input design that processes azimuth sub-looks (AZS), range sub-looks (RGS), and the single look complex (SLC) amplitude image, through parallel branches fused by a spectrum-based attention mechanism. An evaluation on the OpenSARShip dataset demonstrates that LeNet-4SD achieves classification performance comparable to significantly larger benchmark models while being over two orders of magnitude more computationally efficient. To interpret the model's behaviour, Shapley additive explanations (SHAP) was used to quantify the contribution of each data source. The analysis reveals distinct, class-specific dependencies, highlighting the model's reliance on sub-look diversity for container ships and on the high-resolution SLC for tankers. These findings underscore the importance of accounting for processing-induced artefacts and establish that custom, lightweight, multi-input models can be a more effective and efficient strategy for SAR applications than fine-tuning large, pre-trained networks.

item.title: Orientation-Guided Homography for Fine-Grained Cross-View Localization
item.dc:creator: Yangchun Zhang
Xudong Kang
Puhong Duan
Shutao Li
Ze Song
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
item.description: Cross-view localization aims to estimate the precise position and orientation of a ground-view image by aligning it with satellite imagery. However, existing homography-based methods are typically evaluated under limited orientation noise (±45°) and exhibit limited refinement capability, as their local correlation-based refinement relies on a reasonably good initial orientation estimate. To address these limitations, we propose a fine-grained cross-view localization method based on orientation-guided homography (OGH-Net), whose core idea is to predict an initial orientation prior that explicitly guides subsequent homography refinement. Specifically, we first design a hybrid bird’s-eye-view (BEV) transformation to generate BEV images with preserved central geometry and expanded coverage. Then, we introduce a lightweight orientation-prior module that provides a coarse yaw estimate across the full ±180° range. Finally, we develop a multiscale iterative homography module that progressively refines the projection matrix through hierarchical iterations across multiple feature resolutions. Under cross-area, unknown-orientation conditions, it reduces mean localization error by 11% and mean orientation error by 27% on VIGOR, and further reduces mean localization error by 27% on KITTI compared with previous state-of-the-art methods. Moreover, OGH-Net runs in real time at up to 107 FPS on a single RTX 3090 GPU, offering a favorable trade-off between accuracy and efficiency. The code and trained models will be released at https://github.com/YCZhang2025/OGH-Net. Published: 2026-06-02T20:09:12+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.792 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yangchun Zhang; Xudong Kang; Puhong Duan; Shutao Li; Ze Song

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3697650

Relevance: 0.792 (must_read)

Cross-view localization aims to estimate the precise position and orientation of a ground-view image by aligning it with satellite imagery. However, existing homography-based methods are typically evaluated under limited orientation noise (±45°) and exhibit limited refinement capability, as their local correlation-based refinement relies on a reasonably good initial orientation estimate. To address these limitations, we propose a fine-grained cross-view localization method based on orientation-guided homography (OGH-Net), whose core idea is to predict an initial orientation prior that explicitly guides subsequent homography refinement. Specifically, we first design a hybrid bird’s-eye-view (BEV) transformation to generate BEV images with preserved central geometry and expanded coverage. Then, we introduce a lightweight orientation-prior module that provides a coarse yaw estimate across the full ±180° range. Finally, we develop a multiscale iterative homography module that progressively refines the projection matrix through hierarchical iterations across multiple feature resolutions. Under cross-area, unknown-orientation conditions, it reduces mean localization error by 11% and mean orientation error by 27% on VIGOR, and further reduces mean localization error by 27% on KITTI compared with previous state-of-the-art methods. Moreover, OGH-Net runs in real time at up to 107 FPS on a single RTX 3090 GPU, offering a favorable trade-off between accuracy and efficiency. The code and trained models will be released at https://github.com/YCZhang2025/OGH-Net.

item.title: Multi-modal Prompt Tuning with Modified Knowledge Distillation for the Joint Classification of Hyperspectral Image and LiDAR Data
item.dc:creator: Yi Kong
Shaocai Yu
Yuhu Cheng
C. L. Philip Chen
Xuesong Wang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: While pre-trained foundation models (FMs) exhibit remarkable generalization across diverse tasks, their full fine-tuning under limited labeled data remains prohibitively expensive and prone to performance degradation or overfitting. To mitigate these challenges in the context of multimodal remote sensing image classification, we propose the multi-modal prompt tuning with modified knowledge distillation (MPT-MKD), a parameter-efficient learning framework enabling accurate class predictions while updating only a small fraction of model parameters. Prompt tuning is adapted to multimodal remote sensing by injecting modality-specific learnable prompts (spectral and LiDAR prompts) into a student FM, whereby cross-modal interactions are captured through self-attention. In addition, a knowledge distillation scheme is designed that leverages both limited labeled samples and abundant unlabeled data, where a teacher FM, with parameters frozen except for a learnable classifier, generates soft pseudo-labels for the student FM. To mitigate potential inaccuracies in the logits predicted by the teacher FM, a similarity-and class-consistency-based selection strategy is employed to identify a more reliable subset of unlabeled samples and to reconstruct modified soft targets, thereby alleviating the adverse effects of noisy distillation signals. Experimental results on three real HSI and LiDAR datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. Published: 2026-06-02T20:06:09+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.792 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yi Kong; Shaocai Yu; Yuhu Cheng; C. L. Philip Chen; Xuesong Wang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3699347

Relevance: 0.792 (must_read)

While pre-trained foundation models (FMs) exhibit remarkable generalization across diverse tasks, their full fine-tuning under limited labeled data remains prohibitively expensive and prone to performance degradation or overfitting. To mitigate these challenges in the context of multimodal remote sensing image classification, we propose the multi-modal prompt tuning with modified knowledge distillation (MPT-MKD), a parameter-efficient learning framework enabling accurate class predictions while updating only a small fraction of model parameters. Prompt tuning is adapted to multimodal remote sensing by injecting modality-specific learnable prompts (spectral and LiDAR prompts) into a student FM, whereby cross-modal interactions are captured through self-attention. In addition, a knowledge distillation scheme is designed that leverages both limited labeled samples and abundant unlabeled data, where a teacher FM, with parameters frozen except for a learnable classifier, generates soft pseudo-labels for the student FM. To mitigate potential inaccuracies in the logits predicted by the teacher FM, a similarity-and class-consistency-based selection strategy is employed to identify a more reliable subset of unlabeled samples and to reconstruct modified soft targets, thereby alleviating the adverse effects of noisy distillation signals. Experimental results on three real HSI and LiDAR datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

item.title: CLCNet: A Curriculum-Guided Contrastive Learning Network with Spectral-Hierarchical Attention for Hyperspectral-LiDAR Image Classification
item.dc:creator: Changzhe Jiao
Chao Hu
Xu Tang
Hao Zhu
Licheng Jiao
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: The scarcity of labeled samples has long constrained hyperspectral–LiDAR fusion for land-cover classification, making contrastive learning a promising paradigm. However, existing methods still suffer from false negative interference and insufficient modeling of hard negatives, which degrades representation quality. To address these challenges, we propose CLCNet, which enhances contrastive learning from two perspectives. First, a Spectral– Hierarchical Multi-Head Attention Mechanism reorders and groups spectral channels to decouple attention across groups and improve feature discriminability. Second, a curriculum-aware dynamic negative sampling strategy adaptively filters potential false negatives and progressively emphasizes hard negatives, stabilizing convergence and enhancing representation learning. In addition, we propose a Spectral–Hierarchical Dual-Stage Fusion Encoder that incorporates LiDAR edge priors at shallow layers and elevation cues at deeper stages, enabling spatially aware and complementary multimodal fusion. Comprehensive experiments across three public benchmark datasets highlight the robustness and performance advantages of our network. The reference code for our work can be accessed at https://github.com/chaoreee/CLCNet. Published: 2026-06-03T19:40:37+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.791 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Changzhe Jiao; Chao Hu; Xu Tang; Hao Zhu; Licheng Jiao

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3700042

Relevance: 0.791 (must_read)

The scarcity of labeled samples has long constrained hyperspectral–LiDAR fusion for land-cover classification, making contrastive learning a promising paradigm. However, existing methods still suffer from false negative interference and insufficient modeling of hard negatives, which degrades representation quality. To address these challenges, we propose CLCNet, which enhances contrastive learning from two perspectives. First, a Spectral– Hierarchical Multi-Head Attention Mechanism reorders and groups spectral channels to decouple attention across groups and improve feature discriminability. Second, a curriculum-aware dynamic negative sampling strategy adaptively filters potential false negatives and progressively emphasizes hard negatives, stabilizing convergence and enhancing representation learning. In addition, we propose a Spectral–Hierarchical Dual-Stage Fusion Encoder that incorporates LiDAR edge priors at shallow layers and elevation cues at deeper stages, enabling spatially aware and complementary multimodal fusion. Comprehensive experiments across three public benchmark datasets highlight the robustness and performance advantages of our network. The reference code for our work can be accessed at https://github.com/chaoreee/CLCNet.

item.title: SARU: A Shadow-Aware and Removal Unified Framework for remote sensing images with new benchmarks
item.dc:creator: Zi-Yang Bo
Wei Lu
Hongruixuan Chen
Si-Bao Chen
Bin Luo
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Shadows are a prevalent problem in remote sensing imagery (RSI), degrading visual quality and severely limiting the performance of downstream tasks like object detection and semantic segmentation. Most prior works treat shadow detection and removal as separate, cascaded tasks, which can lead to cumbersome process and error accumulation. Furthermore, many deep learning methods rely on paired shadow and non-shadow images for training, which are often unavailable in practice. To address these challenges, we propose Shadow-Aware and Removal Unified (SARU) Framework , a cohesive two-stage framework. First, its dual-branch detection module (DBCSF-Net) fuses multi-color space and semantic features to generate high-fidelity shadow masks, effectively distinguishing shadows from dark objects. Then, leveraging these masks, a novel, training-free physical algorithm (N 2 " role="presentation"> 2 2 SGSR) restores illumination by transferring properties from adjacent non-shadow regions within the single input image. To facilitate rigorous evaluation and foster future work, we also introduce two new benchmark datasets: the RSI Shadow Detection (RSISD) dataset and the Single-image Shadow Removal Benchmark (SiSRB). Extensive experiments on the AISD and RSISD datasets demonstrate that SARU achieves state-of-the-art shadow detection performance with IoU scores of 86.35% and 89.71%, respectively, providing a performance gain of over 3.5% compared to recent methods like CADDN and SDDNet. For shadow removal, our training-free N 2 " role="presentation"> 2 2 SGSR algorithm attains an average processing speed of approximately 1.3s, which is over 10 times faster than the state-of-the-art MAOSD while maintains an SRI value close to 0.9 on both the AISD and SiSRB datasets, a level comparable to the advanced RS-GSSR method. By holistically integrating shadow detection and removal to mitigate error propagation and eliminating the dependency on paired training data, SARU establishes a robust, practical framework for real-world RSI analysis. The source code and datasets are publicly available at: https://github.com/AeroVILab-AHU/SARU . Published: 2026-06-03T16:20:33+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.791 (must_read)

Most prior works treat shadow detection and removal as separate, cascaded tasks, which can lead to cumbersome process and error accumulation. Furthermore, many deep learning methods rely on paired shadow and non-shadow images for training, which are often unavailable in practice. To address these challenges, we propose Shadow-Aware and Removal Unified (SARU) Framework , a cohesive two-stage framework. First, its dual-branch detection module (DBCSF-Net) fuses multi-color space and semantic features to generate high-fidelity shadow masks, effectively distinguishing shadows from dark objects. Then, leveraging these masks, a novel, training-free physical algorithm (N 2 " role="presentation"> 2 2 SGSR) restores illumination by transferring properties from adjacent non-shadow regions within the single input image. To facilitate rigorous evaluation and foster future work, we also introduce two new benchmark datasets: the RSI Shadow Detection (RSISD) dataset and the Single-image Shadow Removal Benchmark (SiSRB). Extensive experiments on the AISD and RSISD datasets demonstrate that SARU achieves state-of-the-art shadow detection performance with IoU scores of 86.35% and 89.71%, respectively, providing a performance gain of over 3.5% compared to recent methods like CADDN and SDDNet. For shadow removal, our training-free N 2 " role="presentation"> 2 2 SGSR algorithm attains an average processing speed of approximately 1.3s, which is over 10 times faster than the state-of-the-art MAOSD while maintains an SRI value close to 0.9 on both the AISD and SiSRB datasets, a level comparable to the advanced RS-GSSR method. By holistically integrating shadow detection and removal to mitigate error propagation and eliminating the dependency on paired training data, SARU establishes a robust, practical framework for real-world RSI analysis. The source code and datasets are publicly available at: https://github.com/AeroVILab-AHU/SARU .

item.title: $A^2$: Smaller Self-Supervised ViTs Localize Better than Larger Ones
item.dc:creator: Sreehari Rammohan
Huy Ha
Carl Vondrick
item.prism:publicationName: arXiv
item.description: Robust visual classification often depends on localizing the main foreground objects in an image while ignoring contextual distractors. Surprisingly, we find that the attention maps of smaller self-supervised ViTs localize foreground objects better than those of larger ViTs. However, we still need large ViTs, because they extract richer representations from each patch. To get the best of both worlds, good localization and rich representations, we propose $A^2$, a simple method that leverages this inverse scaling finding by decoupling where to look (a small attention model) from what to extract (a large embedding model): we crop around the attention peaks of a small model and embed the crops with a larger model. $A^2$ uses entirely pretrained features, requires no group labels, and does not require per-dataset attention or backbone training. Across 5 benchmarks, $A^2$ is competitive with backbone-matched loss-level methods like DFR, and outperforms end-to-end attention training under stronger distribution shifts. Published: 2026-06-02T04:45:02+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.790 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Sreehari Rammohan; Huy Ha; Carl Vondrick

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.790 (must_read)

Robust visual classification often depends on localizing the main foreground objects in an image while ignoring contextual distractors. Surprisingly, we find that the attention maps of smaller self-supervised ViTs localize foreground objects better than those of larger ViTs. However, we still need large ViTs, because they extract richer representations from each patch. To get the best of both worlds, good localization and rich representations, we propose $A^2$, a simple method that leverages this inverse scaling finding by decoupling where to look (a small attention model) from what to extract (a large embedding model): we crop around the attention peaks of a small model and embed the crops with a larger model. $A^2$ uses entirely pretrained features, requires no group labels, and does not require per-dataset attention or backbone training. Across 5 benchmarks, $A^2$ is competitive with backbone-matched loss-level methods like DFR, and outperforms end-to-end attention training under stronger distribution shifts.

item.title: SBCD: Spectral backbone with intensity-structure dual-domain fusion for robust cross-domain few-shot learning
item.dc:creator: Ying Yang
Entong Zhu
Xueying Li
Bin Hu
Kehua Guo
item.prism:publicationName: Pattern Recognition
item.description: Recognition performance in few-shot learning deteriorates sharply when training and testing distributions diverge. The challenge is particularly acute in fine-grained scenarios, where domains differ in imaging conditions, material properties, and sensor characteristics, and only limited labeled data are available. To address this, we propose the Spectral Backbone for Cross-Domain Few-Shot Learning (SBCD). This framework introduces multimodal fusion into Cross-Domain Few-Shot Learning (CD-FSL) to improve robustness and generalization under domain shift. SBCD consists of three core components: Multistage Spectral U-Net (MSU-Net), Intensity and Structure Dual-Domain Fusion (ISDF), and the meta-learning framework M-FDM. MSU-Net generates a compact pseudo-hyperspectral prior from RGB images, providing complementary cross-band cues without requiring real hyperspectral sensors. Building on this prior, ISDF fuses modalities from two complementary perspectives: intensity fusion maintains color and luminance consistency, while structural fusion enhances edge, texture, and orientation information. During meta-training in M-FDM, ISDF combines the two views to stabilize feature learning under sparse supervision and provide more focused class evidence in our analyses. Experiments on standard CD-FSL benchmarks, including CUB, Cars, and Plantae, show that SBCD achieves consistent accuracy improvements, reaching 60.58% in 1-shot and 77.55% in 5-shot settings. Furthermore, introducing hyperspectral cues at meta-training after RGB pretraining yields the best cross-domain generalization among the tested modality schedules. Published: 2026-06-03T15:36:29+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.790 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ying Yang; Entong Zhu; Xueying Li; Bin Hu; Kehua Guo

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.114138

Relevance: 0.790 (must_read)

Recognition performance in few-shot learning deteriorates sharply when training and testing distributions diverge. The challenge is particularly acute in fine-grained scenarios, where domains differ in imaging conditions, material properties, and sensor characteristics, and only limited labeled data are available. To address this, we propose the Spectral Backbone for Cross-Domain Few-Shot Learning (SBCD). This framework introduces multimodal fusion into Cross-Domain Few-Shot Learning (CD-FSL) to improve robustness and generalization under domain shift. SBCD consists of three core components: Multistage Spectral U-Net (MSU-Net), Intensity and Structure Dual-Domain Fusion (ISDF), and the meta-learning framework M-FDM. MSU-Net generates a compact pseudo-hyperspectral prior from RGB images, providing complementary cross-band cues without requiring real hyperspectral sensors. Building on this prior, ISDF fuses modalities from two complementary perspectives: intensity fusion maintains color and luminance consistency, while structural fusion enhances edge, texture, and orientation information. During meta-training in M-FDM, ISDF combines the two views to stabilize feature learning under sparse supervision and provide more focused class evidence in our analyses. Experiments on standard CD-FSL benchmarks, including CUB, Cars, and Plantae, show that SBCD achieves consistent accuracy improvements, reaching 60.58% in 1-shot and 77.55% in 5-shot settings. Furthermore, introducing hyperspectral cues at meta-training after RGB pretraining yields the best cross-domain generalization among the tested modality schedules.

item.title: Spatial-Spectral Enhancement Contrastive Learning Network for Few-Shot Hyperspectral Image Classification
item.dc:creator: Shizhe Zhang
Lina Yang
Yaojun Cai
Yifeng Tan
Wanxing Zhu
Thomas Xinzhang Wu
Xichun Li
Yan Yuan Tang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Few-shot hyperspectral image (HSI) classification plays a crucial role in the field of remote sensing with the rapid development of deep learning. However, few-shot HSI classification remains challenging. When labeled samples are scarce, severe overfitting arises from limited feature generalization ability in effectively capturing and fusing discriminative spatial-spectral features. In this study, a spatial-spectral enhancement contrastive learning network (SSECLN) is proposed for few-shot HSI classification. This network includes three modules: spatial-spectral contrastive learning (SSCL), spatial-spectral cross-attention (SSCA), and partial multikernel convolution (PMC). The SSCL includes multiscale spatial enhancement and bidirectional spectral enhancement (SSE) and batch contrastive learning (BCL). The SSE extracts spatial structural information at different scales and combines forward and backward spectral context to fully explore spectral dependencies. By introducing same-class sample constraints, BCL not only improves intra-class compactness and inter-class separability but also alleviates overfitting problems in few-shot scenarios. The SSCA realizes dynamic interaction of local spatial information with global spectral information, adaptively accommodating the coupled spatial-spectral features through its inherent mechanism. The PMC is used to extract important spatial-spectral features to improve model efficiency. The results show that the overall accuracy is increased by 5.86%-34.59% (IP), 0.96%-24.28% (KSC), 2.39%-23.40% (Salinas), and 3.86%-35.85% (UP) by using the comparison of SSECLN and the state-of-the-art approaches. Codes are available at https://github.com/Zhangshizhe04/SSECLN. Published: 2026-06-02T20:06:09+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.788 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Shizhe Zhang; Lina Yang; Yaojun Cai; Yifeng Tan; Wanxing Zhu; Thomas Xinzhang Wu; Xichun Li; Yan Yuan Tang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3699048

Relevance: 0.788 (must_read)

Few-shot hyperspectral image (HSI) classification plays a crucial role in the field of remote sensing with the rapid development of deep learning. However, few-shot HSI classification remains challenging. When labeled samples are scarce, severe overfitting arises from limited feature generalization ability in effectively capturing and fusing discriminative spatial-spectral features. In this study, a spatial-spectral enhancement contrastive learning network (SSECLN) is proposed for few-shot HSI classification. This network includes three modules: spatial-spectral contrastive learning (SSCL), spatial-spectral cross-attention (SSCA), and partial multikernel convolution (PMC). The SSCL includes multiscale spatial enhancement and bidirectional spectral enhancement (SSE) and batch contrastive learning (BCL). The SSE extracts spatial structural information at different scales and combines forward and backward spectral context to fully explore spectral dependencies. By introducing same-class sample constraints, BCL not only improves intra-class compactness and inter-class separability but also alleviates overfitting problems in few-shot scenarios. The SSCA realizes dynamic interaction of local spatial information with global spectral information, adaptively accommodating the coupled spatial-spectral features through its inherent mechanism. The PMC is used to extract important spatial-spectral features to improve model efficiency. The results show that the overall accuracy is increased by 5.86%-34.59% (IP), 0.96%-24.28% (KSC), 2.39%-23.40% (Salinas), and 3.86%-35.85% (UP) by using the comparison of SSECLN and the state-of-the-art approaches. Codes are available at https://github.com/Zhangshizhe04/SSECLN.
